hook: You already know how to work with vectors in standard coordinates, but the choice of coordinates is actually arbitrary — and the right choice can make a hard problem trivially easy. This unit reveals the rich geometric structure hiding inside every vector space: hidden subspaces, natural decompositions, and flexible ways to 'mod out' by directions you don't care about. What does it really mean to change your perspective on a vector space, and how far can you push that idea?
concept: The unifying idea is that a vector space has many equivalent descriptions, and understanding how those descriptions relate — through bases, subspaces, and quotients — unlocks powerful tools for solving linear systems and understanding linear maps. Formally, this is the study of the internal structure of finite-dimensional vector spaces via the four fundamental subspaces and their cosets.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to express the same vector in different bases and construct the change-of-basis matrix that translates between coordinate representations.
Master how to identify the row space and column space of a matrix and understand how they capture exactly what a linear transformation can 'reach' and 'see.'
Apply the Fundamental Theorem of Linear Algebra to decompose any matrix into four orthogonal subspaces — row space, column space, null space, and left null space — and see how they fit together.
You will learn to describe the complete solution set of an inconsistent or underdetermined system as a coset of a subspace, shifting your view from a single solution to a full geometric object.
Construct quotient spaces by formally collapsing a subspace to a point, gaining a rigorous framework for 'ignoring' certain directions in a vector space and measuring what remains.
